sentences.text: [NON-ENGLISH] yes.
As we were walking, we were guarded very strictly, as I mentioned, with dogs and machine guns.
And they didn't have a way to take us, because there were no concentration camps in this city.
And they took us to a jail overnight.
When we walked into this jail, the Germans told us that they'd be back in the morning.
We didn't know if we have to believe them or not.
But while we were there overnight, we could see in the jail people in cages, in standing cages, so that they couldn't sit down, and sitting cages, so they couldn't get up.
They were not Jews.
They were Poles.
And we didn't know if we were going to be exactly the same way put there.
But in the morning, the Germans arrived.
And on the side, they gave us bread.
They gave us something to drink.
And they took us to work.
And we were very happy, because what-- what work we had, we were doing trenches for the tanks, so when they pass, they would fall in.
And we were laying mines, as well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And those Germans-- after a couple of days, it was just unbelievable, because the way they came to pick us up, we couldn't believe that they were really nice people.
We asked them, why did you come like this to the railway with dogs and machine guns to carry?
So they told us that they were told that they're picking up murderers from the station.
They didn't have a clue who they were picking up.
And this is why they were so strict, until they found out that we are human and found out who we are.
And we never had trouble.
We were there for quite a little while working on those trenches.
And we never had any trouble.
No one was ever hit or anything.
They were quite, quite nice, I must say.
It wasn't a camp.
It was like a farm.
And we slept on straw, which was very good, because it was in fresh air.
It was different.
There were no lice or anything.
It was-- really, we wish we would've stayed there till the end of the war.
But unfortunately, this finished too.
For only a short time.
We were there only a few weeks.
I can't remember exactly how many.
But from there, same story all over again-- back to the carriages and back to a camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So we traveled for a while.
And we arrived to a city called Czestochowa And the camp was called Czestochowianka.
In the same city, there was three camps.
One camp was called Warte, and the other one was called Plzen.
And ours was Czestochowianka.
What was our job?
Mainly, the job was tanks were coming there.
And they were repaired.
And they were repainted.
And other jobs, like odd jobs or-- my job was I wasn't painting the tanks.
I always tried to be in fresh air.
I loved fresh air.
So I found myself-- because I was once working at the railway, so I got a job at the railway again.
And I was unloading things.
And it was a very, very good job.
And trains were passing with potatoes.
I used to steal potatoes.
And then I brought them into the camp.
And we used to cook them.
If I would be caught, most probably, with the potatoes, I could have been shot.
But no one was thinking of those things in the days.
So-- but the camp was quite strict, very strict.
I remember like today a father was there with two sons.
And to sell it to a Pole, what they did-- the [INAUDIBLE] got some hoses joined.
They went and cut this off.
And they wanted to sell, because Poles were making for the shoes' soles.
And they were caught.
And the two boys were hanged.
And they were hanged there for 48 hours in the camp.
And the father was there watching when they hanged those boys.
So it was quite strict in the camp.
And I was there working for quite a while.
Not so long, because from-- from Skarcziska, the other camps, really that I worked, they didn't keep me so long, because the war in Poland was nearly coming to an end.
It was 1944.
And I really can't say much about this camp.
Only I can say that there were Russian soldiers there outside working next to me.
And they told me one day that the war was coming to an end, that the Russians were not far.
And I spread the rumors in the camp.
And everybody was very, very happy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] With food, I must say, everyone was helping himself.
It wasn't so bad.
People were not really dying like that, only those who were caught or killed, but not like in the other camps.
Everyone was really working.
And-- and it wasn't so bad.
It was strict, but not too bad.
The beds were all right, you know.
You could survive.
If they let you stay there, you most probably would survive the war there.
But when the Russians told us this, it didn't take long.
And it was-- they had good information.
And one day, in the morning, we got out from the barracks.
And we could see the whole city was in darkness.
It was daytime.
The Russians ever were coming closer.
They were using some fog or some kind of-- and the whole part of the city was in darkness during the day.
And they were coming through, the Russians.
And we had an order to leave Czestochowa, the camp, as we were working.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, as I mentioned, we had the uniforms painted and the trousers painted.
So I had the jacket painted, as well.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As we were walking, I once-- I mentioned before [NON-ENGLISH].
I could see that the Russians-- what they did, they came, the first offensive.
But they were staying there for a few months.
They didn't go any further.
So I was caught once already, nearly lost my life.
But I knew here it looked [INAUDIBLE] because you could see it as we were walking.
The Ukrainians were guarding us very strictly.
And we could see the Germans running back and forth.
They didn't really know what they were doing.
So I could see that it's really the end, that something is there.
And I knew that I've got my sister and my brothers most probably survived the war.
And then I'm going-- who knows where I'm going now?
So I run in-- I manage to throw my jacket off myself.
And I'm run into a toilet at the station.
I was in the toilet for about five minutes.
No, sorry.
I must say-- no, sorry.
When we were walking, that was when they took us first to a camp called Plzen.
And from there, they walked us to the station after a couple of days.
And there, as we were walking, I just ran into the toilet.
In the toilet, all things come through my mind.
Shall I stay?
Shall I go?
Shall I stay?
And I didn't know what to do with myself.
I thought, if I stay, and the Russians may be not moving any further, I would be killed from the Poles or from the Germans.
If I go, who knows where I'm going?
Maybe I'm going to be gassed.
My mind said to get out.
I walked out.
I went in.
All the people were already-- the doors were closed.
I went to the Ukrainian.
And I told him, look, I've been in the toilet.
Can you let me in?
He opened the door.
He let me in.
Five minutes after, the Germans took over the city.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- and I went.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were driving-- we were on the train for a couple of days.
They took us to Germany.
And the carriage is not ordinary carriages, just carriages that you couldn't survive, a human being.
It's hard to believe they can-- could survive something like that.
When we arrived to Germany-- already in Germany in some city, which I don't remember what city it was-- they opened the tank to catch a bit of fresh air.
They knew that most probably everyone will be dead.
So they opened the doors.
And you wouldn't believe-- German people were coming to us to ask us, what's going on?
Where are they?
Where is the war, what things?
And you know, we were frightened.
We didn't know what to say.
But they didn't know, themselves, what was going on.
They didn't know who we were or what we were, nothing.
So then they closed after a few minutes.
They closed the doors.
And we went further.
We arrived to a place called Buchenwald.
We got off the train.
The Germans were waiting for us with dogs.
And we could see, coming closer to the camp called Buchenwald, huge gates.
They opened the gates.
And the orchestra started to play for us.
We just didn't know what-- what this meant.
You know, you come in-- you're walking into a camp where you see skeletons walking, and an orchestras playing for you.
But we are going to have a bath, they told this.
We didn't know what kind of bath we were going to have, if it's going to be a real bath or not.
We went to have a bath.
Before we went to have the bath, everyone had to get undressed.
Whatever jewelry you had or anything, there were boxes standing there.
You had to throw everything in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] While I throw my things in, I notice a diamond.
I don't know why I did it.
I picked it up and put it in my mouth.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this diamond, as you will hear, later on, survived my life.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When we went in, they shaved us.
And they asked everyone to go into a special water that, in case you had something with you, it would most probably come out.
But I had the diamond in my mouth.
And I kept it in my mouth.
And I went through it.
And then you have to register yourself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So they gave us the stripes-- And I had summer stripes.
That was in winter.
They gave us summer stripes.
You're not allowed to have the buttons on.
You have to be open-- and a hat and a number.
My number was 116,001.
[CRYING] And they were asking to register.
And there were Poles, as well, which they say, if someone is Polish or Christian or something, they can register themselves on the one side, and Jews on the other side.
If I can still remember, I think I was registered as a Pole.
But it didn't help anything.
In the camp later on, they were all the same.
Everyone got a number.
And they were all the same.
I can't remember what-- I think it was red, or whatever it was, and yeah, we were supposed to be Haeftlinge.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then they took us to a barrack.
My barrack was number 55.
And the fella in charge of this barrack, if I can remember, was Gustav.
He was one of the fuhrers there in this.
He was Jewish.
But he looked after all the youngsters.
That was his camp, number 55.
And they had a list of everyone, afterwards.
And if someone was a kapo or a policeman, they had a code, like a code there in the camp-- inside the camp, like it was an underground.
And he belonged to this party, this Gustav.
And those who were not that bad, they were cleaning roofs, the snow down, or other jobs they'd give them.
And those who were really bad, they finished them off in the camp during our time.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in Buchenwald, one thing I must say, cleanliness was in Buchenwald, which we were all full of lice before the disinfection in Buchenwald.
We were covered with thousands of lice.
But in Buchenwald, this was one good thing that we didn't suffer.
But unfortunately, it didn't help, because we didn't have food to eat.
Everyone was-- they had the gas chambers there.
They had everything.
But they didn't need to gas anybody because people were dying without it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I was-- we were lying-- our beds were-- oh, what do you call them?
Bunks?
Bunks, yes.
And there were 15 people in one bunk.
You couldn't sit there or stand there.
You just went into the bed moving in, and you'd-- with your feet first, because you couldn't turn around, with your head looking out.
That's how it was.
And we didn't do anything there.
That's what we had to do all day, is lie in bed.
And that was all.
There was nothing to do.
But you could go down if you wanted to.
You could go down.
Yes, we talked to each other.
Oh, yes, we talked to each other.
But otherwise, there was nothing to do.
In the camp, there was only one thing to talk about-- how you could have a piece of bread.
That was our main subject, unfortunately.
No.
Really-- really, you know, we were alive.
But there was nothing, really, because all we were worrying about-- how we were going to survive.
We knew the war might come soon to an end and everything.
But we knew we were not going to make it.
We didn't think anyone of us is going to make it with all the things that we could see.
For example, the first night when we came, you had to go at night, 7 o'clock at night, out.
You were lucky that you were able to take your blanket, because we had a blanket.
And you stand there.
And by the time the fellow came to count you, it was at least two hours.
And then someone was missing, because-- why was he missing?
He was sitting in the toilet, most probably, and he fell in, because it wasn't an ordinary toilet, you know, a toilet for 20 people or something.
And he fell, alive.
And he fell into the toilet.
It was a ditch with--
Yeah, a ditch.
--seats.
So by the time they found out where the body is, we were there freezing, and people were dying.
And you were not allowed to take the bodies away, even if he fell next to you, because he had to be counted.
And this is what Buchenwald was for me.
But after a few days, I was just lucky, a fellow came in to inspect the dishes-- because Buchenwald, as I mentioned, was very clean.
He came to inspect the dishes.
And the fellow wasn't happy with the dishes.
And we were there in this one room for about 300 people.
So he sacked the fellow who was doing the dishes.
So I said that I wouldn't mind doing the dishes if I could get a bit of extra soup.
He said, yes, if you promise you do a good job, you can have the dishes.
So I did the dishes, so I had extra soup, what was left in the barrel.
And then a few friends could have the soup, too.
I was there with my friend.
Janek [NON-ENGLISH] was there and all the other boys.
But if there was something left, we could have it.
If there was nothing left, well, you always could scrap something, because you didn't leave anything, any drop in the barrel.
But then I found out people were going out from our camp.
It was the same camp.
But we were guarded in a different way.
There were wires to the next.
Ours was barrack 55.
And the others had wires already.
It most probably was 60.
So I found out that they are going to Weimark in the morning.
Early in the morning, a train is going to Weimark with Germans guarding them, and because Weimark was bombed a lot.
And they were looking, cleaning things out, and bury the bodies, or things, whatever, has to be done.
So we had a ball there, because when the bombs were coming down, we didn't-- we were not scared of the bombs.
All we were worrying about was how to find food.
So we were jumping into the houses and stealing food.
And people were killed.
But we didn't worry about those things.
And this is how I had quite a bit to eat.
And I was quite strong, because while I was at the beginning in Buchenwald, I remember like today, I walked up maybe 15 steps, and I fell all the steps down, because I was so weak already.
I couldn't take it anymore.
So I was very happy to be able to do it.
I wasn't allowed, really, to do it.
But I used to do it every day.
One night [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] while I was asleep-- and I was asleep next to Janek-- they came and took me out.
A fellow came and took me out.
And I didn't know what I'd done.
I knew he was going to kill me or something.
But I didn't know why he was taking me.
But what happened-- a day before, I had very-- toothache.
And I couldn't take it anymore.
I just couldn't.
And there was a German dentist in the camp.
And I thought, well-- I was going crazy.
I didn't know what to do.
So I went into this dentist, And I asked him if he could help me.
I was crying.
And he said to sit down.
And he took out my tooth.
And I thought to myself, maybe because of this, what I did, and I wasn't allowed to do it, they're taking me somewhere.
They're going to kill me or gas me.
I didn't know what they were going to do.
But when I came closer, I could see they had quite a few people there, lots of people standing already, waiting.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So from there, they took us to the station again.
So there I'm going again.
The Germans, they really didn't harm us or anything.
We were loaded up there.
And they took us to a place called Flossenburg.
Was your friend with you?
No, my friend-- no.
I was the only one taken, and the other people that were standing, waiting there.
My friend-- that's what hurt me so much, because I was going to lose him, too.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we arrived in a place called Flossenburg.
In Flossenburg, it was not a very big camp.
I would say at most probably 2,000 or 3,000 people there were, no more.
And when we arrived there, they gave us to eat.
It was in the evening.
They gave us to eat.
And I had-- in every camp, I had with me a little spoon.
I used to always in my shoes carry a little spoon, because I was afraid to catch a disease or something.
And you had to be very careful in the camp if you really wanted to look after yourself.
So I was always eating with this spoon.
And while I was eating, the Blockalteste, which is the-- the one who looks--
--after the place, after this barrack, he came to me.
And he asked what am I doing?
Why am I eating with this spoon?
Why I don't use the other spoons from-- I said, look, this is a spoon-- I hope you don't mind, but this is a spoon that I carry from camp to camp.
And I'm eating with this spoon.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So and that's not really true.
I'll tell you the truth.
This little spoon I picked up in Buchenwald.
I had the other spoons in the other camp.
But in Buchenwald, when we were going into this thing, I couldn't take the-- the spoon with me, so I lost it.
But I picked up another one afterwards.
And I was trying to use the one.
But I told him that I'd had this from all the camps.
And he let it go.
The next morning, about 5 o'clock in the morning  or 6:00, he had a fellow who was after him.
And he sent him in to call me.
I asked the boy next to me, I said, look, can you tell me why is he calling me?
So he said, most probably you're going to get 25 lashes because of what happened to you yesterday, because that's what he does if someone is not clean or something.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So I went in.
I knocked at the door.
And he asked me to come in, started talking to me.
And then he says to me to sit and have breakfast.
I just didn't understand why he would.
And then he started talking to me.
And he just befriended himself very much with me.
He didn't tell me who he was first or anything.
But he told me that he would like me to come for lunch, as well, because we were working just outside the camp.
And you could go back into the camp during lunch hour.
And at lunchtime, I was working.
It happened so that I had to work.
And I didn't go to see him.
So he came at night and asked me what happened that I didn't come to see him.
He said he wants me to come every morning for breakfast.
And he wants me to come every dinner to his room-- because he was on his own-- and have dinner with him.
Well, he had a different dinner than I was eating or the other boys were eating.
Different breakfast, too.
He had what the Germans had.
So then he told me who he was.
He was in the camp not as a Jew.
He was as a Christian, but he was Jewish.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we became really good friends.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I had plenty to eat.
I had no problem in this camp till they-- there was bombing.
They were bombing the camp one night.
And then after this, we didn't have any, because what we were doing-- really, building a factory for ammunition.
And as soon as it was going to be finished, the Americans came, and they bombed it.
They marked the sky.
They put signs on the sky where the camp was.
And they were bombing the factory.
And after this, we didn't already have work to do.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They didn't have work to send us.
One day, they sent us into work, and there were young Germans-- their age maybe was 15 or 16-- they were guarding us outside the camp.
And they called us in to remove rocks.
And the rocks could have been as big as the wall here.
And it was impossible to move.
They didn't really need those rocks to move.
But they just wanted to have fun with us.
So they asked us to move the rocks.
So they started hitting us.
And you couldn't hit them back.
Though, you could if you had the strength to do it, most probably.
But you'd be killed for it.
So that's the fun  they had.
So after a few days or so, we were transported again.
We went on a transport.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
From there, they sent us to a place called Mauthausen.
We might just leave it there.
End of tape 4.
